summary: Gruber reviews the third-generation iPhone SE, arguing it serves a crucial role as Apple's backward-compatibility device — not in a technical sense, but in terms of user experience. He contends the Home button interaction model, while clearly inferior to the iPhone X's gestural interface, provides essential familiarity for a large segment of iPhone buyers who prioritize comfort over cutting-edge design. The A15 chip brings meaningful efficiency gains rather than just speed, and the camera benefits from superior image signal processing despite unchanged hardware. He notes the $30 price increase for 5G is unlikely to hurt sales given carrier installment plans. The most interesting question, he argues, is whether Apple will ever transition the SE to the modern interaction model at all.
key_points: The iPhone SE represents 'experience compatibility' — Apple's version of backward compatibility, preserving familiar interaction patterns rather than technical APIs
The A15 chip matters not for speed but for power efficiency, giving SE buyers meaningfully longer battery life over the several-year-old phones they're replacing
The technotariat class misunderstands the SE's audience — these buyers want familiarity and comfort, not the latest design paradigm
The $30 price increase for 5G connectivity won't matter because most SE buyers purchase through carrier installment plans at $12/month

Apple's institutional willingness to break backward compatibility (32-bit apps, Intel to ARM) is what enables bold moves like the iPhone X — but the SE shows Apple also understands when continuity matters
money_quotes: This is backward compatibility Apple-style — not technical compatibility, but experience compatibility.
The iPhone SE is the comfort iPhone.
But here's the thing: some people don't like bold moves, even if they're good.
For some people — many of them, but make no mistake definitely not all, older — familiarity trumps all.
The technotariat class may think it offensive or spiteful or just plain goofy that Apple's entry model iPhone looks and feels half a decade old.
It was a bold move to make a change so fundamental to a product so insanely popular, but clearly the right one.
Like an all-time great athlete, years past their prime, but still pulling their weight on the team, contributing something essential.
Too many nerds have internalized the way it used to be, that when chips got faster they necessarily consumed more power.
key_insight: The iPhone SE is Apple's version of backward compatibility — preserving not technical APIs but the comfort of a familiar interaction model for the enormous segment of users who value familiarity over innovation.
